headline: Story.
Pace.
Image.
blocks.subhead: Illustration has always sat at the centre of how I think visually, from comics and editorial through to album and comic-book covers.

blocks.label: Where it started
heading: Built on
comics.
paragraphs: I grew up on comics, and studied them rather more closely than most readers might. How panels guide the eye, how poses carry story, how colour and contrast make drama. That obsession became a degree in Illustration, with a dissertation on the language of comics and how images tell stories, sometimes without a word.

blocks.text: It turns out the skill travels. Graphic design, animation, branding and UI all rely on the same ideas: clarity, pacing, and making something instantly readable.

blocks.label: Range
heading: The skill
travels.
paragraphs: Over the years that thinking has gone to work for Marvel, Image Comics, IBM and Microsoft.

I’ve also drawn portraits for Prospect Magazine, some of the biggest names in politics and culture, for their recurring Brief Encounter column. Editorial moves quickly and the deadlines are tight, so you learn to catch a personality without overworking the image.

blocks.label: Covers
heading: Loosened up,
on purpose.
paragraphs: Covers are where the work loosens up. Over the years I've contributed to dozens of album and comic covers, work that sits outside client briefs entirely, closer to poster illustration than design.

A record cover has to set the mood before anyone presses play, so type, colour and composition all get to hint at the sound. A comic cover has one job, to sell the issue in half a second on a shelf. Both are a tiny world-building exercise, and both let me push further than most commercial work allows.

items.value: Marvel · Image Comics · IBM · Microsoft · Prospect
